name: shu (air)
shortDescription: Shu is the Egyptian god of air, light, and the space between the earth and the sky. He separates Nut (the sky) and Geb (the earth), maintaining cosmic order.
description: Shu is a primordial Egyptian deity, embodying air, light, and the space separating the earth (Geb) and the sky (Nut). Born from Atum (or Ra in some accounts) through self-creation, Shu is a vital component of the Ennead, the group of nine foundational deities in Egyptian mythology. His primary role is to keep Nut and Geb apart, preventing them from perpetually embracing and thus allowing for creation and life to flourish. Without Shu, the world would be in eternal darkness and chaos. He is often depicted as a man wearing an ostrich feather on his head, symbolizing lightness and emptiness. Shu's efforts maintain Ma'at, the concept of cosmic order, truth, and justice. He represents the life-giving breath and the sunlight that penetrates the atmosphere. He is sometimes described as tiring from his task of separating Nut and Geb, which explains the occasional storms and disturbances in the sky. Shu's influence extends to the pharaoh, who was seen as the earthly representative of Ma'at, and thus relied on Shu's power to maintain order and stability in the kingdom. He is also associated with dryness and light, countering the watery chaos that existed before creation.
